title: Christians could force Trump to be an abolitionist of abortion with merely a lift of the finger. #YouDontGetMyVoteYET
author: Nathan Weisser
orientation: An April 2024 op-ed urging evangelical Christians to publicly withhold their votes from Donald Trump until he supports the abolition of abortion, arguing that their leverage as a constituency could force his hand. Aimed primarily at evangelical voters who feel obligated to back Trump as the lesser evil.
audiences: Evangelical Trump voters wrestling with his abortion stance
Abolitionists strategizing how to pressure politicians
Pro-life activists weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
Pastors and church leaders shaping political conscience
Political observers tracking the 2024 evangelical bloc

questions.question: What did Trump actually say about abortion that prompted this article?
questions.audience: Political observers tracking the 2024 evangelical bloc
questions.answer: Trump issued a statement saying abortion policy should be decided by each state through vote or legislation, framing the issue as fundamentally about the will of the people. The author reads this as a functionally pro-choice position that hands the decision back to voters and ultimately to individual mothers.
questions.quote: “The states will determine by vote or legislation or perhaps both, and whatever they decide must be the law of the land. Many states will be different. Many will have a different number of weeks or some will [be] more conservative than others, and that’s what they will be. **At the end of the day, this is all about the will of the people.**” – Donald Trump

questions.question: Why does the author say the Pro-Life movement is itself pro-choice?
questions.audience: Pro-life activists weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Weisser argues that mainstream pro-life advocacy only attacks particular methods of abortion while always leaving the act itself up to the mother. Because the movement refuses to call for the mother to face the just penalty for murder, it functionally preserves choice rather than abolishing it.
questions.quote: This is no surprise, though, because in a sense, the Pro-Life movement is itself pro-choice, because although they want to slowly chip away at *methods,* they will always leave the choice of *abortion itself* up to the mother, prohibiting her from receiving the just penalty for her murderous actions.

questions.question: Why is Trump willing to defy his evangelical base on abortion?
questions.audience: Political observers tracking the 2024 evangelical bloc
questions.answer: The author claims Trump knows evangelicals are too afraid of another Biden term to actually withhold their votes. He can therefore make pro-choice-flavored statements with confidence that they will grumble but still turn out for him.
questions.quote: It’s simple, he knows they’ll bite their tongues and go along with it anyways, because they’re too afraid of four more years of Biden.

questions.question: What is the #YouDontGetMyVoteYET strategy?
questions.audience: Abolitionists strategizing how to pressure politicians
questions.answer: Weisser proposes that evangelicals publicly tell Trump he has not yet earned their vote and will not get it unless he supports the abolition of abortion before November. The point is to use the prophetic voice of the church to leverage Trump's need for the evangelical bloc.
questions.quote: So, I humbly propose a mission for evangelicals. #YouDontGetMyVoteYET. Trump has plenty of time until November, and if we were to *actually use* the prophetic voice of the church toward the abolition of abortion, he would have no choice but to listen.

questions.question: What scriptural authority does the author appeal to for confronting political leaders?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders shaping political conscience
questions.answer: He invokes Proverbs 21:1, which speaks of God turning the heart of kings, together with the Great Commission in Matthew 28:19-20 to teach the nations all that Christ commanded. He frames evangelical silence as a failure to exercise authority Christ has already granted.
questions.quote: We have the authority, through Christ as our head, to turn the heads of kings. (Proverbs 21:1) We have been commanded to exercise that authority in the Great Commission, by teaching the nations all God has commanded. (Matthew 28:19-20)

questions.question: Why shouldn't Christians settle for 'some progress is better than no progress' under Trump?
questions.audience: Evangelical Trump voters wrestling with his abortion stance
questions.answer: The author says Trump's recent comments make clear there would be no abolition progress in a second term because he believes the current legal status of abortion is already optimal. Settling for the status quo therefore amounts to no progress at all.
questions.quote: Yet, Trump’s recent statements have proven that without any correction, there *would* be no progress towards abolition under his second term! He believes that the legal state of abortion at this moment is the optimal one.

questions.question: Who does the author say is really to blame for America's spiritual peril?
questions.audience: Pastors and church leaders shaping political conscience
questions.answer: Not Biden, Harris, or Pelosi, but Christians themselves who have failed to tear down the high places. He frames evangelical apathy toward abortion as the central reason the country teeters over judgment.
questions.quote: They don’t understand that this country hangs by a thread over the fiery pits of destruction, not because of Biden, or Kamala Harris, or Nancy Pelosi, but because *we* *Christians* have failed to tear down the high places in our land.

questions.question: What concrete message does the author want Christians to deliver to Trump?
questions.audience: Abolitionists strategizing how to pressure politicians
questions.answer: He calls for a unified evangelical statement telling Trump he has not earned their vote and will lose in November unless he supports abolition. The framing is conditional support with time still on the clock for him to repent.
questions.quote: So, we need to be saying with one unified voice, “Trump, you haven’t earned our vote. You **will not** win in November, unless you do what’s right and support abolition. You still have plenty of time to earn it.”

related.title: How Shall an Abolitionist Vote?
related.reason: Direct companion piece responding to the same Trump statement and wrestling with the abolitionist voting dilemma.

related.title: Why I Believe Voting for a Pro-Abortion Candidate is a Sin, No Matter the Context
related.reason: Same author Nathan Weisser pushing the lesser-of-two-evils argument to its biblical conclusion.

related.title: Together, We CAN Affect the Ballot Initiative Votes
related.reason: Weisser's later strategic playbook for evangelical electoral leverage on abortion-related ballots.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Foundational explainer for the article's claim that the Pro-Life movement is functionally pro-choice.

related.title: The Inexhaustible Cowardice of Oklahoma Senate President Greg Treat
related.reason: Parallel case study of political cowardice on abortion that abolitionists have called out by name.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Concrete example of the pro-life-as-pro-choice critique applied to elected officials.
